title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 02

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Mariam al-Khatib, “Learning and Exchange across the Sahara,” modern historical interpretation, 2019.
“Trans-Saharan networks were not simply routes for gold, salt, and enslaved people. They also carried scholars, books, legal ideas, and religious practices. Cities such as Timbuktu and Gao became important because commercial exchange and Islamic learning reinforced one another.”
Source 2: Historian Daniel Mercer, “States of the Western Sudan,” modern historical interpretation, 2022.
“The growth of states in West Africa depended on rulers’ ability to tax and protect trade. Islam could strengthen royal legitimacy, but the wealth of states such as Mali came primarily from controlling commercial routes and resources rather than from cultural exchange alone.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of trans-Saharan networks.
(b) Describe one specific historical development that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way trans-Saharan networks were similar to or different from another exchange network during c. 1200–1450.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing cultural and religious exchange while Source 2 emphasizes state power, taxation, and control of trade.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant development, such as the growth of Mali, Mansa Musa’s pilgrimage, the spread of Islam in West Africa, Timbuktu’s scholarly community, gold-salt trade, caravan routes, or rulers’ taxation of merchants.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another network, such as the Indian Ocean, Silk Roads, Mediterranean, or Swahili Coast, using specific evidence about merchants, states, religion, technology, or goods.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should avoid treating trans-Saharan trade as only an economic system or only a religious exchange. The best responses explain how commerce, scholarship, and political authority could reinforce one another. For part (c), a strong comparison names another network and explains a specific similarity or difference, such as the role of monsoon winds in the Indian Ocean or the role of caravan technology in Saharan travel.

questions.prompt:
Source: Petition from free people of color in a Caribbean port city to a colonial assembly, 1791. This excerpt is original but modeled on petitions from the era of Atlantic revolutions.
“We have paid taxes, served in the militia, purchased property, and defended this colony in moments of danger. Yet men who have done none of these things claim rights that are denied to us because of ancestry and complexion. We do not ask for disorder, nor do we seek to overturn property. We ask that the principles now spoken of in the empire—liberty, citizenship, and equal law—be applied to loyal subjects who have proven their usefulness.”
(a) Identify the likely audience of the petition.
(b) Explain how the petition reflects one broader idea associated with Atlantic revolutions.
(c) Explain one limitation of the petition as evidence for the goals of all people living in Caribbean colonial societies.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible audience, such as a colonial assembly, imperial officials, white property-holding elites, or political authorities responsible for citizenship and legal rights.
(b) 1 point: Explains a broader revolutionary idea reflected in the petition, such as natural rights, equality before the law, citizenship, representative government, or challenges to inherited legal privilege.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid limitation, such as the petition representing free property-holding people of color rather than enslaved people, women, poor laborers, or all colonized groups, or noting that the petition defends property and order rather than calling for full social revolution.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the source’s point of view. The petitioners present themselves as loyal, property-owning, taxpaying, and useful because they are trying to persuade authorities. Strong answers should also recognize that Atlantic revolutionary language could be used by different groups for different goals, and that not all groups sought the same degree of social change.

questions.prompt:
Source: Description of a political map of the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, produced for a European newspaper in 1922.
The map shows the former Ottoman territories divided into new zones after World War I. Anatolia is labeled “Turkish National Government,” while Syria and Lebanon are shaded as a French mandate. Iraq, Transjordan, and Palestine are shaded as British mandates. The map marks new borders, railway lines, oil fields near Mosul, and port cities along the eastern Mediterranean. A caption below the map states: “The settlement of the Ottoman lands after the Great War: mandates, resources, and routes.”
(a) Identify one political change shown in the described map.
(b) Explain one way World War I contributed to the political changes shown in the map.
(c) Explain one way the settlement shown in the map could have contributed to later regional tensions.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a political change, such as the breakup of the Ottoman Empire, the creation of mandate territories, new borders, European control over former Ottoman lands, or the emergence of a Turkish nationalist government.
(b) 1 point: Explains how World War I contributed to the changes, such as Ottoman defeat, Allied wartime agreements, peace settlements, European strategic interests, or the use of mandates under the League of Nations.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid later tension, such as borders that grouped diverse communities, foreign control over strategic routes or oil, competing nationalist claims, resentment toward mandates, or conflict over Palestine.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should connect the map’s visual details to historical causation. The oil fields, railways, ports, mandate labels, and borders all suggest that the postwar settlement involved both political restructuring and strategic interests. Strong answers should avoid saying only that “new countries were created” and should explain why the settlement produced conflict or resentment.
